meta.title: Demonstrate understanding of usability in human‑computer interfaces
meta.purpose: Students are able to demonstrate understanding of usability in human-computer interfaces.

grade_descriptors.N1: Evidence does not reach the minimum threshold for Achieved‑level indicators.
grade_descriptors.N2: Some Achieved‑level indicators are met, but not enough to satisfy all required Achieved criteria.
grade_descriptors.A3: All Achieved‑level indicators are met; no Merit‑level indicator meets the Merit threshold.
grade_descriptors.A4: All Achieved indicators are met and at least one Merit indicator is met, but overall performance is below the Merit threshold.
grade_descriptors.M5: All Achieved indicators are met and the Merit threshold is reached; no Excellence‑level indicator meets the Excellence threshold.
grade_descriptors.M6: All Achieved indicators are met, all Merit indicators are met, and at least one Excellence indicator is met, but overall performance is below the Excellence threshold.
grade_descriptors.E7: All Achieved and Merit indicators are met and the Excellence threshold is reached, but not all Excellence indicators are met.
grade_descriptors.E8: All Achieved, Merit, and Excellence indicators are met.

omis.description: Describes the purpose of human‑computer interfaces

positive_signals: Explicit statement of user achievement (e.g., 'helps students book library laptops')
Specific purpose description (e.g., 'lets users order food for delivery')
Clear interface function (e.g., 'enables account creation')
Direct benefit to user (e.g., 'facilitates online shopping')
negative_signals: Vague phrases like 'it is useful' without specifics
No mention of what the interface achieves
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Purpose must be clearly stated in terms of user actions or goals, not just general usefulness.

success_examples: The website helps students book library laptops.
The app lets users order food for delivery.

omis.description: Identifies and describes usability principles used in an interface

positive_signals: Names a principle and describes its use (e.g., 'uses consistency in menu layout')
Links principle to specific interface feature
Provides example of principle application (e.g., 'feedback with loading spinner')
Explains how principle is implemented
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Simply naming a usability principle is insufficient; must describe how it is used in the interface.

success_examples: It uses consistency so all pages have the same menu layout.
The system uses feedback by showing a loading spinner when pages are loading.

omis.description: Explains how usability principles have been applied in the interface

positive_signals: Links named principle to specific feature (e.g., 'help popup explains login')
Describes how principle prevents issues (e.g., 'disables submit until fields filled')
Connects principle to user behavior or outcome
Explains application with examples
negative_signals: Mentions feature without linking to principle
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Explaining the definition of a usability principle is not the same as showing how it has been applied in the interface.

success_examples: Help and documentation is provided through a 'Need Help?' popup that explains how to log in.
Error prevention is used by disabling the 'Submit' button until all fields are filled.

omis.description: Explains how the interface’s usability aids the user experience

positive_signals: Describes how features support user experience (e.g., 'clean layout finds form quickly')
Links usability to benefits like clarity, speed, confidence
Explains impact on user (e.g., 'feedback confirms booking success')
Connects principle application to user outcomes
negative_signals: Vague benefits (e.g., 'makes it better for users')
No connection to user experience or specific aids
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Must specify how usability features aid the user experience, not just that they do.

success_examples: The clean layout means users can find the reservation form quickly.
Feedback messages make it clear when a booking was successful.

omis.description: Compares the usability of human‑computer interfaces

positive_signals: Contrasts interfaces using principles (e.g., 'larger fonts improve readability')
Compares specific features linked to usability
Highlights differences in principle application
Uses usability terms in comparison
negative_signals: Lists differences without linking to usability
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Comparison must tie specific differences to usability principles, not just any observed differences.

success_examples: Interface A used larger fonts and was easier to read than Interface B.
Both had search bars, but only Interface B used auto-complete to help users.

omis.description: Judges which interface used usability principles more effectively and explains why

positive_signals: States which interface is better and reasons with principle (e.g., 'consistent layout builds confidence')
Judges effectiveness using usability principles
Explains superiority with specific examples
Links judgment to user benefits via principles
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Judgment must be supported by how usability principles are applied, not personal preference.

success_examples: Interface B was better because its consistent layout helped users feel confident.
Interface A used clearer feedback messages, so users knew their form was submitted.

omis.description: Suggests multiple usability‑based improvements for the interface

positive_signals: Proposes concrete improvements linked to principles (e.g., 'progress bar for feedback')
Multiple suggestions with usability ties
Specific changes (e.g., 'larger icons for accessibility')
Explains how improvement enhances usability
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Each suggested improvement must be linked to a usability principle to be counted.

success_examples: Add a progress bar to improve feedback.
Use larger icons to help users with visual impairments.
